raw_case:
A unique presentation of subcutaneous Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum infection: A case report of a diagnostic challenge


Introduction

Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum is a yeast-like anamorphic rare fungus commonly found in tropical areas. This case report is the first one located in South America.

Case report

A 67-year-old patient presented with a 5-year history of right foot pain attributed to foot trauma while at sea 5 years prior. During surgical exploration, an impressive whitish cerebriform-like cyst was encountered. Genetic analysis using the genes ITS1, ITS4, LSU-R, and LSU-F was performed, and a phylogenetic tree identified C. debeurmannianum.

Conclusion

A complete characterization of this fungus which causes human disease has not yet been achieved and more research is needed.1

Introduction

Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum is a rare fungus belonging to the genus Cutaneotrichosporon and order Trichosporonales. Its taxonomic classification has presented some challenges; however, in recent years, it has been clarified through phylogenetic analysis [1]. These yeast-like anamorphic organisms are commonly encountered in nature, primarily in tropical areas. They can occasionally be found as part of the human microbiota or may transiently colonize the respiratory tract or skin [2]. A comprehensive understanding of this fungus has yet to be achieved in the scientific literature, particularly regarding the diagnostic approach and the diverse clinical manifestations of human disease. Existing articles describe cases of fever, urinary tract infection, septicemia, subcutaneous cysts, onychomycosis, and diabetic foot infection caused by this organism [[3], [4], [5], [6]].To our knowledge, this is the first case report describing a unique and impressive presentation of a subcutaneous cerebriform-like cyst caused by this fungus in an immunocompetent patient. Additionally, is the first case report of C. debeurmannianum subcutaneous infection in South America, specifically in Ecuador. Therefore, our contribution is significant in helping to understand this rare infection and offers valuable insights to the scientific community. This case report was prepared following the CARE Guidelines [7].2

Case presentation

A 67-year-old male presented to the emergency department with fluctuating pain in the sole of his right foot for 5 years, which had intensified over the past two weeks. He attributed the onset of the symptoms to a traumatic foot injury sustained while in the sea five years prior. His personal history is significant for long-standing hypertension under treatment and prostatic hyperplasia managed with prostatectomy. Familial history is relevant for a mother with breast cancer.Physical examination revealed swelling, erythema, heat, and pain in an area 3cm in diameter on the sole of the right foot. Purulent drainage was noted upon applying pressure. The patient was afebrile and showed no signs of disseminated infection. The remainder of the examination was normal.Laboratory results indicated normocytic normochromic anemia: red blood cell count 4.5 x 10^6/μL (normal range 5–6.5), hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL (normal range 14.5–18.5), hematocrit 44.1 % (normal range 45–55), mean corpuscular volume of 95.7 umᶾ (normal range 80–100), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 31.8 g/dl (normal range 30–36).Under spinal anesthesia, exploration, and surgical debridement were performed, involving the removal of inflammatory tissue, areas of necrosis, and pus. A sample of the purulent drainage was collected and sent for culture. Unexpectedly, a 2 cm whitish, cyst-like structure was discovered, featuring a cerebriform inner and outer surface, suggestive of fungal etiology (Fig. 1). The cyst and surrounding tissue were excised for histopathological examination. The wound was cleaned with an antiseptic solution and covered with an antibiotic dressing. The patient was discharged the next day with an antibiotic treatment and was monitored closely, showing no complications.One day after discharge, the culture of the purulent drainage revealed coagulase-positive Staphylococcus aureus, which was sensitive to multiple antibiotics, including those the patient was already taking. As a result, no additional treatment was necessary.Histopathological examination of the cerebriform cyst revealed a macroscopic saccular formation measuring 2 x 1 × 1 cm, with an internal surface that was whitish and rough, and an external surface that was whitish and smooth (Fig. 2a). Microscopically, the cyst wall exhibited keratinized stratified squamous epithelium without adnexa, along with a granular layer and lumen filled with keratin fibers (Fig. 2b). Focally, the stroma showed neovascularization, and a dense diffuse inflammatory infiltrate composed of polymorphonuclear cells, lymphocytes, and rounded structures, some of which were pigmented, suggesting fungal spores (Fig. 2c). Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining highlighted the apparent fungal spores, with no hyphae observed (Fig. 2d).Histopathological examination of the tissue revealed four irregular fragments of soft whitish tissue, slightly indurated, measuring between 0.5 and 1.2 cm (Fig. 3a). Microscopically the tissue exhibited an acanthotic epidermis with focal parakeratosis and compact hyperkeratosis, as well as spongiosis and reactive hyperplasia of the basal cells. The dermis showed a proliferation of capillaries with open lumens, while the stroma displayed an increase in soft fibroblasts, the presence of epithelioid cells, and a severe inflammatory infiltrate consisting of mostly polymorphonuclear, followed by lymphocytes, plasma cells, and occasional foreign body-type giant cells. Multiple spherical structures were identified amid the cellular debris; with no hyphae detected (Fig. 3b). Groccot-Gomori methenamine silver stain revealed the presence of fungal spores, which were strongly stained black throughout the dermis (Fig. 3c).Based on the context of this patient—immunocompetent, with a longstanding duration of symptoms, cerebriform-like cyst extraction, and inconclusive histopathological examination—the specimen was sent for genetic analysis to achieve a definitive diagnosis.Samples (two cut sections, each less than 80 mm) were treated with xylene to completely remove paraffin, followed by two washes with 1000uL of molecular grade absolute ethanol at room temperature. The ethanol wash involved adding ethanol to samples and mixing by vortexing until the tissue turned opaque. A centrifugation step was then performed for 2 min at maximum speed to pellet the tissue, after which the ethanol was removed and discarded without disturbing the pellet. High-speed centrifugation was conducted additionally to remove any residual ethanol. The samples were air-dried for 20 min at room temperature. Next, 100uL of digestion buffer and 4uL of protease were added to each sample. The tubes were mixed to ensure complete immersion of the samples, and a pipette tip was used to push the tissue into the solution. For digestion, the samples were incubated for 16h at 50 °C in a dry bath. After incubation, the samples were clarified. The isolation additive was prepared by mixing 120ul of reagent with 275uL of molecular grade absolute ethanol. This isolation additive was added to the sample tubes and the solution was mixed by pipetting. The sample solution was then transferred to a filter cartridge placed in a collection tube. The filter was centrifuged at 10000×g for 30 seconds. The collection tube was discarded and replaced by a new one for the washing steps, which involved 700uL of wash buffer 1 (centrifuged 10000×g for 30 seconds) and 500uL of wash 2/3 (centrifuged 10000×g for 30 seconds). After each wash, the collection tubes were replaced with new ones. A final spin of 30 seconds was performed to discard any excess wash buffer in the filter column. Finally, 60uL of preheated (95 °C) elution buffer was added to the column, incubated at room temperature for 1 minute, and centrifuged at maximum speed for 1 minute. Isolated DNA was stored at −20 °C until used in the subsequent amplification steps.Identification was performed using the genes ITS1 (CTTGGTCATTTAGAGGAAGTAA) and ITS4 (TCCTCCGCTTATTGATATGC). PCR parameters were 35 cycles with an annealing temperature of 52 °C for 30 seconds, initial denaturation at 95 °C for 5 seconds, followed by denaturation at 94 °C for 30 seconds, and extension at 72 °C for 1 minute. The final extension lasted 4 minutes, following a previous protocol [8]. For LSU-F (GTACCCGCTGAACTTAAGC) and LSU-R (TCCTGAGGGAAACTTCG), another study indicated an annealing temperature of 50 °C for 45 seconds [9]. After conducting a BLAST search, a 100 % match with NG058998.1 C. debeurmannianum was identified. A phylogenetic tree was constructed using muscle alignment and neighbor-joining with 1000 iterations (Fig. 4).3

Discussion

Reviewing the scientific literature, we identified 12 reported cases of C. debeurmannianum detected in human samples. The associated clinical conditions include 2 cases of septicemia, 2 cases of fever, 5 cases of urinary tract infection, 1 case of diabetic foot infection, 1 case of subcutaneous foot cyst, and 1 case of onychomycosis. Additionally, there is a report of 2 bronchial samples; however, it is unclear whether these were associated with human disease. These cases were reported in India, London (in a Nigerian patient), Korea, and Japan (in a Dutch patient) [[3], [4], [5], [6],10]. Here we present the first case report of subcutaneous cyst infection caused by C. debeurmannianum from the region of South America in Ecuador.The previous history that the patient describes of trauma while barefoot at sea on the coast of Ecuador is important for some reasons. It is described that these organisms are distributed in nature, especially in tropical and temperate areas such as the coast of this country, and additionally in seawater [2]. There is a study in which Cutaneotrichosporon dermatis is isolated from soft corals on Brazilian reefs [11]. Another case of subcutaneous infection described a patient with a history of walks on a Nigerian beach [4].It is well known that immunosuppression is a risk factor for acquiring fungal infections [12]. However, in this case, the patient does not have any relevant data that would suggest immunosuppression, as in the other case in an immunocompetent Nigerian patient [4].The cyst removed from our patient is notable, measuring 2cm in diameter and displaying a white, cerebriform-like outer surface. This appearance initially led the team to consider a diagnosis of Coelomycetes infection, as there was a reported case of a similar cyst in a 57-year-old woman in France [13]. On the other hand, in the Nigerian patient, they initially considered a diagnosis of eumycetoma, with the cyst measuring 4 mm × 4 mm. Interestingly, both patients had a similar long-time history of symptoms: our patient experienced symptoms for 5 years, while the Nigerian patient had symptoms for 4 years [4].In the preoperative laboratory exams of our patient, we found normocytic normochromic anemia, which, in the context of a chronic infection, could suggest an anemia of chronic disease (ACD). Fungal infections are known to cause ACD; however, there are no prior reports of C. debeurmannianum being associated with ACD [14]. The literature lacks information regarding the histopathological characteristics of C. debeurmannianum infection or the cyst itself. Here, we describe the main microscopic findings of the cyst and the surrounding tissue. It is important because, although histopathological examination is not an exact method, it serves as a valuable diagnostic support tool.Based on genetic analysis, the genus Trichosporon was reclassified into three genera: Cutaneotrichosporon, Apiostrichum, and Trichosporon. C. debeurmannianum and other members of the genus Cutaneotrichosporon have been reported as pathogens in humans; however, there are no reports of molecular identification of virulence factors associated with these organisms. Previous studies indicate a low minimal inhibitory concentration for antifungal agents in most isolates included in that case series [5].In this case, the treatment was a localized surgical procedure to clean the area and remove the cyst. We did not consider systemic or localized antifungal drugs, as the patient was immunocompetent and only exhibited localized symptoms. In the follow-up examination, he showed no complications.One limitation of our case report is that we only cultured the purulent exudate collected intraoperatively, while the entire cyst was sent directly for histopathological analysis. It would have been valuable to characterize the behavior of this fungus in a medium.There is still insufficient research describing all the aspects of C. debeurmannianum infection in humans. Here, we present a case of a 67-year-old male with a history of foot trauma at the sea and 5 years of fluctuant pain, who developed an impressive cerebriform-like cyst caused by C. debeurmannianum infection. Identifying fungal species in patients is crucial for improving access to molecular tests. In cases like this, collaboration with a multidisciplinary team is essential for achieving a comprehensive management of the patient.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Agustin N. Posso: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Alvaro A. Perez-Meza: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Paul Marquez: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Daniel Garzon-Chavez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization.

Conflict of interest

There are none.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 67-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Fluctuating right foot pain for 5 years, recently intensified
- **History of Present Illness:** Onset of symptoms after traumatic foot injury sustained while at sea 5 years ago
- **Past Medical History:** Long-standing hypertension under treatment, and prostatic hyperplasia managed with prostatectomy
- **Personal History:** None specified
- **Family History:** Mother with a history of breast cancer
- **Physical Examination:** 3 cm area of swelling, erythema, heat, pain, and purulent drainage on the sole of the right foot; remainder of the examination normal
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Laboratory findings: Normocytic normochromic anemia, with slight reduction in RBC count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit.
  - Histopathological analysis: 
    - Whitish cyst-like structure (2 cm) with cerebriform surface composed of keratinized stratified squamous epithelium and dense inflammatory infiltrate with fungal spores (no hyphae) confirmed by PAS and Grocott-Gomori silver stains.
  - Microbiology: Culture of purulent drainage grew Staphylococcus aureus.
  - Genetic analysis (ITS1, ITS4, LSU-F, LSU-R genes): Identified fungal DNA with 100% match to Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial considerations included bacterial infection, eumycetoma, or fungal cyst:**
   - Symptoms of fluctuating pain, erythema, and purulent drainage were consistent with a possible abscess or bacterial infection such as Staphylococcus aureus, which was cultured. However, the unusual cerebriform, cyst-like structure with fungal spores identified on histopathology prompted further investigation.
   - Eumycetoma was considered given the subcutaneous lesion and chronic nature but lacked supporting evidence of grain production or fungal hyphae.
   - Based on histological findings (granular material, fungal spores), fungal etiology was pursued.

2. **Exclusion of alternative fungal diagnoses:**
   - Differential included fungal species like Candida or Aspergillus, typically pathogenic in immunocompromised patients. However, the patient was immunocompetent, and genetic analysis ruled out related species.

3. **Confirmation of Diagnosis:**
   - Genetic sequencing demonstrated 100% genetic alignment with C. debeurmannianum, confirming the rare fungal etiology of the cyst.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Subcutaneous Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum infection presenting as a cerebriform-like cyst in the sole of the right foot.

This diagnosis was supported by the chronic history of fluctuating symptoms following the trauma, histopathology showing fungal spores, and definitive identification through genetic analysis.

---

results.content:
### Reasoning:

**Step 1:** Analyze the patient's chief complaint and history of present illness.

The patient is a 67-year-old male who has been experiencing fluctuating right foot pain for 5 years, which has recently intensified. The onset of symptoms occurred after a traumatic foot injury sustained while at sea five years ago. This suggests that the current condition may be related to the previous injury, possibly involving chronic infection or complications from the trauma.

**Step 2:** Review the past medical history.

The patient has a history of long-standing hypertension, which is being treated, and prostatic hyperplasia managed with a prostatectomy. These conditions may not directly relate to the current foot issue but could influence general health and healing processes.

**Step 3:** Examine the physical findings.

There is a 3 cm area of swelling, erythema, heat, pain, and purulent drainage on the sole of the right foot. The rest of the examination is normal. This localized inflammation with purulent drainage indicates an infective process, likely bacterial in nature, given the presence of Staphylococcus aureus in the culture.

**Step 4:** Interpret the laboratory findings.

The patient has normocytic normochromic anemia with a slight reduction in RBC count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit. This could be due to chronic disease or inflammation, which is common in longstanding infections.

**Step 5:** Analyze the histopathological findings.

A whitish cyst-like structure measuring 2 cm with a cerebriform surface is observed, composed of keratinized stratified squamous epithelium and a dense inflammatory infiltrate containing fungal spores (no hyphae), confirmed by PAS and Grocott-Gomori silver stains. This suggests a fungal component to the infection.

**Step 6:** Consider the microbiology culture results.

The purulent drainage cultured Staphylococcus aureus, indicating a bacterial infection. However, the histopathology shows fungal spores, suggesting a possible mixed infection or a fungal etiology in addition to the bacterial component.

**Step 7:** Evaluate the genetic analysis.

The genetic analysis using ITS1, ITS4, LSU-F, LSU-R genes identified fungal DNA with a 100% match to Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum. This is a newly recognized fungal genus, and its involvement in human disease is relatively understudied.

**Step 8:** Synthesize the information.

The patient has a history of traumatic foot injury followed by chronic foot pain and recent exacerbation with signs of inflammation and purulent drainage. The presence of both bacterial (Staphylococcus aureus) and fungal (Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum) pathogens suggests a complex infection, possibly involving a superimposed bacterial infection on a fungal etiology.

**Step 9:** Consider differential diagnoses.

Given the presentation, differential diagnoses could include chronic osteomyelitis, diabetic foot infection (though diabetes is not mentioned), fungal osteomyelitis, or a foreign body reaction. However, the presence of both bacterial and fungal elements, along with the histopathological findings, points towards a fungal infection complicated by bacterial superinfection.

**Step 10:** Formulate the final diagnosis.

Considering the chronic nature of the symptoms, the history of trauma, the physical examination findings, laboratory results, histopathological analysis, microbiology culture, and genetic analysis, the most appropriate diagnosis is chronic cutaneous and subcutaneous infection due to Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum with superimposed Staphylococcus aureus bacterium.

### Answer:

Chronic cutaneous and subcutaneous infection due to Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum with superimposed Staphylococcus aureus bacterium

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Initial considerations included bacterial infection, eumycetoma, or fungal cyst based on symptoms of fluctuating pain, erythema, and purulent drainage, as well as histopathological findings of a cerebriform, cyst-like structure with fungal spores.
<Step 2> Eumycetoma was considered due to the subcutaneous lesion and chronic nature but was excluded due to the absence of grain production or fungal hyphae.
<Step 3> Fungal etiology was pursued based on histological findings of granular material and fungal spores.
<Step 4> Alternative fungal diagnoses, such as Candida or Aspergillus, were excluded because the patient was immunocompetent and genetic analysis ruled out related species.
<Step 5> Genetic sequencing demonstrated 100% genetic alignment with C. debeurmannianum, confirming the rare fungal etiology of the cyst.

<Step 6> Final diagnosis: Subcutaneous Cutaneotrichosporon debeurmannianum infection presenting as a cerebriform-like cyst in the sole of the right foot, supported by chronic symptoms, histopathological findings, and genetic analysis.
